query: If a theoretical 1 MW CCGT located in Houston with a 6500 Btu/kWh heat rate and gas price purchase at Henry Hub prices is completely out for maintenance in April and October but available at other times and only runs when it is economical to do so (i.e. market prices are greater than marginal fuel cost), how much would it have made in ERCOT's day-ahead market assuming 2024 prices vs 2023 prices?
final_answer:
## Results: Theoretical 1 MW CCGT in ERCOT Day-Ahead Market (Houston Hub)

### Assumptions
- **Plant**: 1 MW CCGT, heat rate 6,500 Btu/kWh
- **Fuel**: Henry Hub monthly average spot price (same price all hours of the month)
- **Marginal fuel cost** = 6,500 Btu/kWh × HH price ($/MMBtu) ÷ 1,000 = **6.5 × HH price** ($/MWh)
- **Dispatch logic**: Runs only when day-ahead LMP > marginal fuel cost
- **Maintenance outages**: All of April and all of October — zero revenue in those months
- **Simplification**: No VOM ($/MWh), no start-up costs, no min-run constraints — pure spark-spread dispatch

### Sources
- **ERCOT Day-Ahead LMPs**: `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table, Houston hub, `is_day_ahead = true` (internal database)
- **Henry Hub prices**: EIA monthly spot prices ([eia.gov/dnav/ng/hist/rngwhhdm.htm](https://www.eia.gov/dnav/ng/hist/rngwhhdm.htm))

---

## Bottom Line

| Metric | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Net Revenue** | **$342,611** | **$109,538** |
| Hours available (excl. maintenance) | 7,296 | 7,320 |
| Hours run (LMP > fuel cost) | 6,043 | 5,664 |
| Run % of available hours | 82.8% | 77.4% |
| Capacity factor (all hours) | 69.0% | 64.5% |
| Average LMP (all hours) | $57.29/MWh | $28.27/MWh |
| Avg marginal fuel cost | $16.50/MWh | $14.28/MWh |
| Avg spark spread when running | $56.70/MWh | $19.34/MWh |

**2023 revenue was ~3.1× higher than 2024.**

---

## What Drove the Difference?

### 1. August 2023 heat wave dominated everything
August 2023 alone produced **$184,758** — **54% of the entire year's revenue** — in a single month. The Houston day-ahead LMP averaged $265/MWh that month with a max of $4,188/MWh. The plant ran 719 out of 744 hours. 70 hours saw LMPs above $1,000/MWh, contributing $151,329 of revenue. 

August 2024, by contrast, averaged just $35.37/MWh and generated only $16,727 (15% of the year).

### 2. Summer 2023 vs. Summer 2024
- **June–September 2023**: $297,396 (86.8% of annual revenue)
- **June–September 2024**: $42,154 (38.5% of annual revenue)

2023 had a hot, tight summer market; 2024 had mild summer pricing despite similar gas prices.

### 3. Gas prices were lower in 2024
Henry Hub averaged **$2.54/MMBtu in 2023** vs. **$2.21/MMBtu in 2024**. Lower gas = lower marginal cost, which *should* help a CCGT. But the collapse in power prices (avg LMP fell from $57 to $28/MWh) more than offset that advantage.

### 4. Winter 2024 was more profitable than winter 2023
January 2024 produced $18,154 (vs $4,604 in Jan 2023), driven by a cold snap. May 2024 also outperformed May 2023 ($25,301 vs $12,686). But these couldn't compensate for the missing summer price spikes.

---

## Monthly Detail

| Month | 2023 Revenue | 2024 Revenue | Gas '23 | Gas '24 | Avg LMP '23 | Avg LMP '24 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $4,604 | $18,154 | $3.27 | $3.18 | $24.85 | $42.48 |
| Feb | $5,095 | $3,412 | $2.38 | $1.72 | $21.92 | $14.93 |
| Mar | $8,782 | $8,556 | $2.31 | $1.49 | $26.45 | $20.68 |
| **Apr** | **$0** | **$0** | $2.16 | $1.60 | $25.37 | $24.46 |
| May | $12,686 | $25,301 | $2.15 | $2.12 | $30.94 | $47.41 |
| Jun | $43,546 | $11,893 | $2.18 | $2.54 | $74.60 | $32.63 |
| Jul | $30,916 | $7,229 | $2.55 | $2.07 | $58.10 | $22.90 |
| **Aug** | **$184,758** | $16,727 | $2.58 | $1.99 | $265.07 | $35.37 |
| Sep | $38,177 | $6,305 | $2.64 | $2.28 | $69.91 | $23.14 |
| **Oct** | **$0** | **$0** | $2.98 | $2.20 | $34.33 | $26.55 |
| Nov | $10,228 | $7,169 | $2.71 | $2.12 | $31.16 | $22.87 |
| Dec | $3,820 | $4,793 | $2.52 | $3.01 | $20.46 | $24.62 |

---

### Caveats
- Uses **monthly average** Henry Hub prices applied uniformly to all hours — real intra-month gas price variation (especially during extreme events) could shift results
- No basis differential between Henry Hub and Houston Ship Channel gas — in reality, there can be a few cents of basis
- No variable O&M (~$2–4/MWh), no start-up costs, no emissions costs
- Perfect foresight assumed — the plant always knows the day-ahead price before committing
- Revenue is **energy-only** (gross margin above fuel); no ancillary services or capacity payments

GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', timestamp)
ORDER BY month

2. Gas purchase at Henry Hub monthly spot prices
3. Marginal fuel cost ($/MWh) = Heat Rate (Btu/kWh) × Gas Price ($/MMBtu) / 1000
4. For each hour, if the day-ahead LMP > marginal fuel cost AND plant is not in maintenance (April & October), the plant runs and earns revenue = LMP - marginal fuel cost (per MWh), so for 1 MW = revenue per hour = max(0, LMP - fuel_cost)
5. Sum these up for 2023 and 2024.

So: 
- April & October: 0 revenue (plant is out)
- Other months: revenue = max(0, LMP - fuel_cost) for each hour

The fuel cost is: 6500 Btu/kWh × gas_price ($/MMBtu) / 1,000,000 Btu/MMBtu = 6500 × gas_price / 1000000

Wait: 1 MMBtu = 1,000,000 Btu. So 6500 Btu/kWh = 0.0065 MMBtu/kWh. 

Fuel cost ($/MWh) = 0.0065 MMBtu/kWh × gas_price ($/MMBtu) × 1000 kWh/MWh = 6.5 × gas_price ($/MWh)

So: Marginal fuel cost ($/MWh) = 6.5 × Henry Hub monthly price ($/MMBtu).

Let me now run the Python analysis.

Gas prices for each month:
2023: Jan 3.27, Feb 2.38, Mar 2.31, Apr 2.16, May 2.15, Jun 2.18, Jul 2.55, Aug 2.58, Sep 2.64, Oct 2.98, Nov 2.71, Dec 2.52
2024: Jan 3.18, Feb 1.72, Mar 1.49, Apr 1.60, May 2.12, Jun 2.54, Jul 2.07, Aug 1.99, Sep 2.28, Oct 2.20, Nov 2.12, Dec 3.01

Marginal fuel cost = 6.5 × gas_price

Let me compute this in Python.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load the ERCOT day-ahead LMP data
df = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_204511.csv')
df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])
df['year'] = df['timestamp'].dt.year
df['month'] = df['timestamp'].dt.month
df['hour'] = df['timestamp'].dt.hour

# Henry Hub monthly spot prices ($/MMBtu) from EIA
henry_hub = {
    2023: {1: 3.27, 2: 2.38, 3: 2.31, 4: 2.16, 5: 2.15, 6: 2.18, 7: 2.55, 8: 2.58, 9: 2.64, 10: 2.98, 11: 2.71, 12: 2.52},
    2024: {1: 3.18, 2: 1.72, 3: 1.49, 4: 1.60, 5: 2.12, 6: 2.54, 7: 2.07, 8: 1.99, 9: 2.28, 10: 2.20, 11: 2.12, 12: 3.01}
}

# CCGT parameters
heat_rate = 6500  # Btu/kWh
# Fuel cost ($/MWh) = Heat Rate (Btu/kWh) * Gas Price ($/MMBtu) / 1,000,000 * 1000
# = 6500 * gas_price / 1000 = 6.5 * gas_price
# Fuel cost multiplier: 6.5

df['gas_price'] = df.apply(lambda row: henry_hub[row['year']][row['month']], axis=1)
df['marginal_fuel_cost'] = 6.5 * df['gas_price']  # $/MWh

# Plant is out in April (4) and October (10)
df['maintenance'] = df['month'].isin([4, 10])

# Plant runs when LMP > marginal fuel cost AND not in maintenance
df['runs'] = (~df['maintenance']) & (df['lmp'] > df['marginal_fuel_cost'])

# Revenue = (LMP - marginal fuel cost) per MWh, for 1 MW plant, 1 hour => $/h
df['revenue'] = np.where(df['runs'], df['lmp'] - df['marginal_fuel_cost'], 0.0)

# Aggregate by year
yearly = df.groupby('year').agg(
    total_revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    total_hours=('runs', 'sum'),
    total_hours_year=('timestamp', 'count'),
    avg_lmp_when_running=('lmp', lambda x: x[df.loc[x.index, 'runs']].mean()),
    avg_marginal_cost=('marginal_fuel_cost', 'mean')
).round(2)

print("=== YEARLY SUMMARY ===")
print(yearly)
print()

# Monthly breakdown
monthly = df.groupby(['year', 'month']).agg(
    monthly_revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    hours_run=('runs', 'sum'),
    total_hours=('timestamp', 'count'),
    gas_price=('gas_price', 'first'),
    marginal_fuel_cost=('marginal_fuel_cost', 'first'),
    avg_lmp=('lmp', 'mean'),
    avg_lmp_when_running=('lmp', lambda x: x[df.loc[x.index, 'runs']].mean() if df.loc[x.index, 'runs'].sum() > 0 else np.nan)
).round(2)

print("=== MONTHLY BREAKDOWN 2023 ===")
print(monthly.loc[2023].to_string())
print()
print("=== MONTHLY BREAKDOWN 2024 ===")
print(monthly.loc[2024].to_string())
print()

# Some additional stats
for yr in [2023, 2024]:
    d = df[df['year'] == yr]
    total_hrs = len(d)
    running_hrs = d['runs'].sum()
    maint_hrs = d['maintenance'].sum()
    available_hrs = total_hrs - maint_hrs
    run_pct = running_hrs / available_hrs * 100
    capacity_factor = running_hrs / total_hrs * 100
    
    print(f"\n=== {yr} DETAILS ===")
    print(f"Total hours in year: {total_hrs}")
    print(f"Maintenance hours (April + October): {maint_hrs}")
    print(f"Available hours: {available_hrs}")
    print(f"Hours run: {running_hrs}")
    print(f"Run % of available hours: {run_pct:.1f}%")
    print(f"Capacity factor (of total hours): {capacity_factor:.1f}%")
    print(f"Total revenue: ${d['revenue'].sum():,.2f}")
    
    # Average revenue per running hour
    avg_rev_per_run_hr = d.loc[d['runs'], 'revenue'].mean()
    print(f"Avg revenue per running hour: ${avg_rev_per_run_hr:.2f}")
    
    # Overall average LMP
    print(f"Overall average LMP: ${d['lmp'].mean():.2f}/MWh")
    print(f"Average marginal fuel cost: ${d['marginal_fuel_cost'].mean():.2f}/MWh")

steps.content: Now let me verify some key numbers and produce a clean final summary:
steps.reasoning:
Let me verify these results and also look at some key details. Let me double check the data and the logic.

The approach is:
- 1 MW CCGT with 6500 Btu/kWh heat rate
- Gas priced at Henry Hub monthly average prices
- Marginal fuel cost = 6500 * gas_price_$/MMBtu / 1000 = 6.5 * gas_price $/MWh
- Plant is completely out in April and October
- Plant runs only when day-ahead LMP > marginal fuel cost
- Revenue = (LMP - marginal fuel cost) * 1 MW * 1 hour

The key findings:
- 2023: $342,611 revenue, running 6,043 hours out of 7,296 available (82.8%)
- 2024: $109,538 revenue, running 5,664 hours out of 7,320 available (77.4%)

But wait, for 2023, the August revenue is enormous: $184,758. Let me verify August 2023 more carefully because there was an extreme heat event. The LMP average was $265/MWh in August.

Let me also verify the 2024 leap year (8784 hours) vs 2023 (8760 hours) - yes 2024 is a leap year.

Let me also check if there's a slight error: for 2023 November, the count is 721 hours, and for 2024 November it's also 721 hours, while for 2023 December it's 744 - that seems right for DST transitions.

Actually wait - November having 721 hours doesn't make sense. Let me check: November has 30 days, so 30*24 = 720 hours. But DST "fall back" adds an extra hour. In 2023, DST ended on Nov 5 (clocks went from 2am to 1am, so hour 1:00-2:00 appears twice). That would give 721 hours. Yes, that's correct. And for March 2023, 743 hours (spring forward loses an hour from 744). That checks out.

The results look reasonable. Let me now present these clearly.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

df = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_204511.csv')
df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])
df['year'] = df['timestamp'].dt.year
df['month'] = df['timestamp'].dt.month

# Henry Hub monthly spot prices ($/MMBtu) from EIA
henry_hub = {
    2023: {1: 3.27, 2: 2.38, 3: 2.31, 4: 2.16, 5: 2.15, 6: 2.18, 7: 2.55, 8: 2.58, 9: 2.64, 10: 2.98, 11: 2.71, 12: 2.52},
    2024: {1: 3.18, 2: 1.72, 3: 1.49, 4: 1.60, 5: 2.12, 6: 2.54, 7: 2.07, 8: 1.99, 9: 2.28, 10: 2.20, 11: 2.12, 12: 3.01}
}

df['gas_price'] = df.apply(lambda row: henry_hub[row['year']][row['month']], axis=1)
df['marginal_fuel_cost'] = 6.5 * df['gas_price']  # $/MWh
df['maintenance'] = df['month'].isin([4, 10])
df['runs'] = (~df['maintenance']) & (df['lmp'] > df['marginal_fuel_cost'])
df['revenue'] = np.where(df['runs'], df['lmp'] - df['marginal_fuel_cost'], 0.0)

# Verify August 2023 in detail
aug23 = df[(df['year']==2023) & (df['month']==8)]
print("AUGUST 2023 STATS:")
print(f"  Hours: {len(aug23)}")
print(f"  Hours run: {aug23['runs'].sum()}")
print(f"  Avg LMP: ${aug23['lmp'].mean():.2f}")
print(f"  Max LMP: ${aug23['lmp'].max():.2f}")
print(f"  Avg marginal cost: ${aug23['marginal_fuel_cost'].mean():.2f}")
print(f"  Revenue: ${aug23['revenue'].sum():,.2f}")
print()

# Check how often LMP < 0 (prices can go negative in ERCOT)
for yr in [2023, 2024]:
    neg = df[(df['year']==yr) & (df['lmp'] < 0)]
    print(f"{yr}: Hours with negative LMP: {len(neg)}")
    
print()

# What % of revenue comes from summer (Jun-Sep)?
for yr in [2023, 2024]:
    d = df[df['year']==yr]
    summer = d[d['month'].isin([6,7,8,9])]
    total_rev = d['revenue'].sum()
    summer_rev = summer['revenue'].sum()
    print(f"{yr}: Summer (Jun-Sep) revenue: ${summer_rev:,.2f} = {summer_rev/total_rev*100:.1f}% of total ${total_rev:,.2f}")
    
    # Aug alone
    aug = d[d['month']==8]
    aug_rev = aug['revenue'].sum()
    print(f"{yr}: August alone: ${aug_rev:,.2f} = {aug_rev/total_rev*100:.1f}%")

print()

# Check how many hours LMP > $1000 (price cap events)
for yr in [2023, 2024]:
    d = df[df['year']==yr]
    high = d[d['lmp'] > 1000]
    print(f"{yr}: Hours with LMP > $1000: {len(high)}, total revenue from those hours: ${d.loc[high.index, 'revenue'].sum():,.2f}")

print()

# Non-maintenance months only for clarity
print("=== SUMMARY BY MONTH (non-maintenance months only) ===")
monthly = df.groupby(['year', 'month']).agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    hours_run=('runs', 'sum'),
    total_hours=('timestamp', 'count'),
    gas_price=('gas_price', 'first'),
    marginal_cost=('marginal_fuel_cost', 'first'),
    avg_lmp=('lmp', 'mean'),
).round(2)

for yr in [2023, 2024]:
    print(f"\n--- {yr} ---")
    print(f"{'Mo':>3s} {'Gas $/MMBtu':>12s} {'MargCost $/MWh':>14s} {'Avg LMP $/MWh':>14s} {'Hrs Run':>8s} {'Revenue':>12s}")
    for mo in range(1, 13):
        row = monthly.loc[(yr, mo)]
        print(f"{mo:3d} {row['gas_price']:12.2f} {row['marginal_cost']:14.2f} {row['avg_lmp']:14.2f} {int(row['hours_run']):8d} {row['revenue']:12,.2f}")

steps.content:
## Results: Theoretical 1 MW CCGT in ERCOT Day-Ahead Market (Houston Hub)

### Assumptions
- **Plant**: 1 MW CCGT, heat rate 6,500 Btu/kWh
- **Fuel**: Henry Hub monthly average spot price (same price all hours of the month)
- **Marginal fuel cost** = 6,500 Btu/kWh × HH price ($/MMBtu) ÷ 1,000 = **6.5 × HH price** ($/MWh)
- **Dispatch logic**: Runs only when day-ahead LMP > marginal fuel cost
- **Maintenance outages**: All of April and all of October — zero revenue in those months
- **Simplification**: No VOM ($/MWh), no start-up costs, no min-run constraints — pure spark-spread dispatch

### Sources
- **ERCOT Day-Ahead LMPs**: `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table, Houston hub, `is_day_ahead = true` (internal database)
- **Henry Hub prices**: EIA monthly spot prices ([eia.gov/dnav/ng/hist/rngwhhdm.htm](https://www.eia.gov/dnav/ng/hist/rngwhhdm.htm))

---

## Bottom Line

| Metric | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Net Revenue** | **$342,611** | **$109,538** |
| Hours available (excl. maintenance) | 7,296 | 7,320 |
| Hours run (LMP > fuel cost) | 6,043 | 5,664 |
| Run % of available hours | 82.8% | 77.4% |
| Capacity factor (all hours) | 69.0% | 64.5% |
| Average LMP (all hours) | $57.29/MWh | $28.27/MWh |
| Avg marginal fuel cost | $16.50/MWh | $14.28/MWh |
| Avg spark spread when running | $56.70/MWh | $19.34/MWh |

**2023 revenue was ~3.1× higher than 2024.**

---

## What Drove the Difference?

### 1. August 2023 heat wave dominated everything
August 2023 alone produced **$184,758** — **54% of the entire year's revenue** — in a single month. The Houston day-ahead LMP averaged $265/MWh that month with a max of $4,188/MWh. The plant ran 719 out of 744 hours. 70 hours saw LMPs above $1,000/MWh, contributing $151,329 of revenue. 

August 2024, by contrast, averaged just $35.37/MWh and generated only $16,727 (15% of the year).

### 2. Summer 2023 vs. Summer 2024
- **June–September 2023**: $297,396 (86.8% of annual revenue)
- **June–September 2024**: $42,154 (38.5% of annual revenue)

2023 had a hot, tight summer market; 2024 had mild summer pricing despite similar gas prices.

### 3. Gas prices were lower in 2024
Henry Hub averaged **$2.54/MMBtu in 2023** vs. **$2.21/MMBtu in 2024**. Lower gas = lower marginal cost, which *should* help a CCGT. But the collapse in power prices (avg LMP fell from $57 to $28/MWh) more than offset that advantage.

### 4. Winter 2024 was more profitable than winter 2023
January 2024 produced $18,154 (vs $4,604 in Jan 2023), driven by a cold snap. May 2024 also outperformed May 2023 ($25,301 vs $12,686). But these couldn't compensate for the missing summer price spikes.

---

## Monthly Detail

| Month | 2023 Revenue | 2024 Revenue | Gas '23 | Gas '24 | Avg LMP '23 | Avg LMP '24 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $4,604 | $18,154 | $3.27 | $3.18 | $24.85 | $42.48 |
| Feb | $5,095 | $3,412 | $2.38 | $1.72 | $21.92 | $14.93 |
| Mar | $8,782 | $8,556 | $2.31 | $1.49 | $26.45 | $20.68 |
| **Apr** | **$0** | **$0** | $2.16 | $1.60 | $25.37 | $24.46 |
| May | $12,686 | $25,301 | $2.15 | $2.12 | $30.94 | $47.41 |
| Jun | $43,546 | $11,893 | $2.18 | $2.54 | $74.60 | $32.63 |
| Jul | $30,916 | $7,229 | $2.55 | $2.07 | $58.10 | $22.90 |
| **Aug** | **$184,758** | $16,727 | $2.58 | $1.99 | $265.07 | $35.37 |
| Sep | $38,177 | $6,305 | $2.64 | $2.28 | $69.91 | $23.14 |
| **Oct** | **$0** | **$0** | $2.98 | $2.20 | $34.33 | $26.55 |
| Nov | $10,228 | $7,169 | $2.71 | $2.12 | $31.16 | $22.87 |
| Dec | $3,820 | $4,793 | $2.52 | $3.01 | $20.46 | $24.62 |

---

### Caveats
- Uses **monthly average** Henry Hub prices applied uniformly to all hours — real intra-month gas price variation (especially during extreme events) could shift results
- No basis differential between Henry Hub and Houston Ship Channel gas — in reality, there can be a few cents of basis
- No variable O&M (~$2–4/MWh), no start-up costs, no emissions costs
- Perfect foresight assumed — the plant always knows the day-ahead price before committing
- Revenue is **energy-only** (gross margin above fuel); no ancillary services or capacity payments
